steps.description: Perform a clean-up facing operation on the right end of the 65mm diameter bar to establish the Z=0 reference point. Since the raw material length is 150mm, remove minimal material (approx. 0.5mm).
Rough and finish turn the entire length of the bar from 65mm diameter down to the maximum part diameter of 60mm.
Turn the diameter to 55mm starting from the right face (Z0) to a depth of 115mm (Z-115).
Turn the diameter to 45mm from the right face (Z0) to a depth of 85mm (Z-85). This leaves the 20mm wide Ø55 section intact.
Create the recessed groove feature by turning the diameter to 45mm between Z-105 and Z-115. This establishes the 10mm wide Ø45 step between the Ø60 and Ø55 sections.
Turn the diameter to 35mm from the right face (Z0) to a depth of 25mm (Z-25).
Turn the diameter to 30mm for a length of 40mm, specifically between Z-25 and Z-65. This leaves the 15mm wide Ø35 section intact.
Turn the final step to 20mm diameter from the right face (Z0) to a depth of 10mm (Z-10).
Apply a 2mm chamfer at the shoulder of the Ø35 section (Z-10) and a 1mm chamfer at the shoulder of the Ø45 section (Z-65) to break sharp edges as per drawing specifications.

all_reasoning_process:
The process planning begins with a detailed analysis of the engineering drawing. The part consists of seven distinct cylindrical sections with varying diameters (60, 45, 55, 45, 30, 35, 20 mm) and lengths (35, 10, 20, 20, 40, 15, 10 mm), totaling 150 mm. This matches the raw material length, necessitating careful facing to avoid shortening the part beyond tolerance. 

The machining strategy follows the principle of machining from the largest diameter to the smallest to maintain workpiece rigidity and minimize deflection. 
1. **Facing**: Establish a datum on the right face.
2. **Primary Turning**: Reduce the raw 65mm stock to the 60mm base diameter.
3. **Step Turning Logic**: We work from the right end (tailstock side) towards the chuck. We first turn the 55mm diameter for the relevant length, then the 45mm diameter. A specific 'groove' feature (Ø45) is identified between the Ø60 and Ø55 sections, which is machined using step turning. 
4. **Internal Steps**: The Ø30 section is smaller than the Ø35 section to its right, requiring a specific step turning operation between Z-25 and Z-65. 
5. **Finishing**: The Ø20 section is turned last. 
6. **Chamfering**: Finally, the 1mm and 2mm chamfers are applied at the specified transitions. 
Note: Tapering was analyzed but found unnecessary as all transitions are 90-degree steps or chamfers. Drilling is also not required as there are no internal holes.
step_by_step_plan:
1. Secure the 65mm x 150mm cast iron bar in the CNC lathe chuck, ensuring sufficient clamping force for a 150mm extension (use a tailstock center if necessary for stability).
2. Load the Facing Tool and perform a light facing cut at 800 RPM on the exposed end to create a flat reference surface (Z=0).
3. Switch to the Turning Tool. Set speed to 1000 RPM and turn the entire outer diameter to 60mm.
4. Using the same tool, perform step turning to reduce the diameter to 55mm for a length of 115mm from the right face.
5. Continue step turning to reduce the diameter to 45mm for a length of 85mm from the right face.
6. Move the tool to Z-105 and turn the 10mm wide section (Z-105 to Z-115) down to 45mm diameter, creating the step between the Ø60 and Ø55 sections.
7. Turn the diameter to 35mm for the first 25mm of the part length (Z0 to Z-25).
8. Move the tool to Z-25 and turn the section between Z-25 and Z-65 down to 30mm diameter.
9. Turn the final 10mm of the part (Z0 to Z-10) down to 20mm diameter.
10. Load the Chamfering Tool. Set speed to 800 RPM. Apply a 2mm x 45-degree chamfer on the edge of the Ø35 section at Z-10. Apply a 1mm x 45-degree chamfer on the edge of the Ø45 section at Z-65.
11. Perform a final inspection of all diameters and linear dimensions against the drawing before removing the part from the chuck.
